And the rule that saves the most grief: if retrieval finds nothing similar enough, the model isn't called at all; the answer is that it isn't in the documentation.

Leave it instrumented from day one. Log the question, which fragments were used, the answer, the cost and the time, plus a thumbs up or down from the user. Every real failure goes into the evaluation set, and that's where the system genuinely starts improving.

And say out loud what I would NOT do: no agent, no fine-tuning, and no orchestration framework unless they already run one in-house. Knowing how to turn down the fashionable pieces is, to me, half the answer.

en.text: Notice how little technology there is in that answer and how much ordering. That's deliberate, and it's what I think is being measured: that you start from the problem and from how it's measured rather than from the tools; that you can build something simple before something impressive; and that you're able to say no to half the words that were in the job ad.
And the other seven, answered

items: When it makes things up. Three layers, from the outside in: make retrieval find better, because most inventions are gaps the model fills; make the instruction force it to cite and to say "I don't know"; and a third one almost nobody mentions, which I find the most effective: if no fragment clears a minimum similarity, the model isn't called at all. It's cheaper and more honest.

RAG or fine-tuning. The rule in one line: if what's missing is information, RAG; if what's wrong is the way it answers — tone, format, length — fine-tuning. Plus a warning that scores points: never use fine-tuning to load data that changes, because every change forces you to retrain and wait. They can be combined, but you start with RAG.

How to chunk the documents. Cut along the document's own structure — headings, sections, clauses — not blindly every N characters; with a small overlap so a sentence isn't split in half; and store in each fragment where it came from and how recent it is. The test I use is simple: if you read a fragment on its own and it makes no sense without the rest, it was cut wrong.

How I measure. With the three metrics above, against a set of cases that stays untouched, run on every change like any other test. I'd add two nuances that tend to land well: the automated judge has to be validated by reviewing a sample by hand, because the judge gets things wrong too; and the comparison that matters is never against zero, it's against yesterday's version.

Agent or fixed workflow. Fixed workflow by default, always. An agent only when you can't know the steps or their order in advance. The reason is boring and practical: every tool you hand it is one more surface to go wrong, more latency and more spend, and a fixed workflow gets debugged while an agent gets investigated.

Cost and latency. Cost is calls times tokens, so you bring it down in three places: trim the context you send, cache what repeats, and use a small model wherever the big one adds nothing. Latency is almost never in retrieval, it's in the model; you disguise it by streaming the answer as it's generated, and you actually fix it by shortening what you send.

What broke on you. No technique helps here: you tell a real one, with what failed, how it was spotted and what changed afterwards so it wouldn't happen again. And if you don't have one yet, say so plainly and tell the closest thing you've lived through. Inventing a war story is the fastest way to make it obvious you weren't in the war.

en.text: And I'll finish with the answer that has served me best in any interview of my life, and which is worth double here: "I don't know, and this is how I'd find out". In a trade that consists precisely of not trusting what sounds right, saying that doesn't count against you; it's a live demonstration that you know how to work.
The take-home test they'd give me

The test, from what I could find, is nearly always the same one in different costumes: they hand you a pile of documents and ask for a system that answers questions about them. Sometimes live, with someone watching; sometimes to take home, with a weekend to do it. And here's the part I find most interesting, though now I'm giving an opinion: a working system shouldn't be what scores, because almost anyone can produce that today. What sets you apart is everything around it.

items: That you hand in, alongside the code, a set of questions with their expected answers. That is, that you measured rather than merely tried it out.
That the system can say "this isn't in the documents" instead of inventing something. I'd assume there's a question in there with no answer, planted on purpose, and I'd prepare the system for it.
That every answer says which document it came from, so it can be checked.
That you explain what you decided and what you ruled out: why that chunk size, why that model, why you didn't reach for an agent.
That you talk about cost and about what happens if the model service goes down.

And something that's much appreciated and almost nobody does: saying frankly what still doesn't work in what you handed in. Showing the limits of what you built is the clearest sign that you understand what you built.

en.text: Two things I've been told that I'd now know how to answer

The first was said to me word for word: "build me a RAG with my documents to train the model". It sounds reasonable and it mixes two things that have nothing to do with each other. RAG trains nothing. The model stays exactly as it was; what you do is find the fragments of your documents that are relevant and paste them into the question, so it answers by reading them. It's an open-book exam: the student hasn't studied any more, you've just put the book in front of them.
RAG does this without modifying the underlying LLM. Fine-tuning, by contrast, requires adjusting the weights and parameters of the model.

Actual training — fine-tuning — does change the model internally, costs considerably more, and serves a different purpose: teaching it to behave a certain way, not giving it new information. The rule I use is simple: if what's missing is information, RAG; if what's wrong is the way it answers, fine-tuning. And for almost everything I've been asked for so far, the answer was RAG.

The second thing does more damage: "it's six hours of work, you don't even need to know how to code any more". The first half is true. A system that answers questions about your documents and works in the demo can be built in an afternoon, and whoever says it isn't lying: they've seen it. What they haven't seen is the following week. There's an academic paper that studied systems of this kind already running, and it reached a conclusion I find devastating.
Validation of a RAG system is only feasible during operation, and its robustness evolves rather than being designed in at the start.
en.source: Barnett, Kurniawan, Thudumu, Brannelly and Abdelrazek · Seven Failure Points When Engineering a Retrieval Augmented Generation System, 2024

en.text: Read that slowly, because it answers the six-hours line better than any argument could: you can't know whether this works until it's running against real questions, and soundness is earned over time, not drawn on a whiteboard on day one. The six hours are real. What isn't real is that the work is in those six hours.
What an AI engineer expects from the company

So far I've talked about what gets asked of the engineer. The other half is missing, and it's the half that almost never gets written down: what the company has to put in for that work to be possible at all. I'll say it plainly, because I think it's the most useful part of this note: when an AI project fails, often neither the model nor the person who built it was at fault — one of these eight things was missing from day one.

items: Business documentation, not just technical documentation. A model can't explain how a surcharge is calculated if that only lives in one person's head in the accounts department. Before architecture, the business rules need to be written down somewhere. And if they aren't, writing them is the project's first task, not a prerequisite somebody has to hand you finished.

Access to real data, and permission to use it. Without genuine examples — questions real customers asked, documents actually in use — nothing can be evaluated. Working with invented sample data gives a false sense that everything works, and then produces the worst kind of surprise: the one that shows up on launch day.

Someone who decides what a correct answer is. This isn't a technical decision and it isn't mine to make. I need a named person — from business, product or support — who reviews examples and says "this one yes, this one no". If nobody wants to sign off on that criterion, the project isn't ready to start.

One chosen use case, not a list of twenty. Chip Huyen flags this as one of the most expensive mistakes: crowdsourcing ideas across the organisation and ending up chasing many low-impact applications. One case that matters, finished and measured, beats fifteen half-done pilots whose only outcome is concluding that "AI didn't work here".

A clear position on data and privacy, in writing. Which information may go out to an external provider, which may not, what has to be anonymised and who answers for it. You have that conversation with legal at the start or you have it late, and late means rebuilding the whole system.

A visible, accepted budget. Every model call costs money, and the cost grows with usage. The company has to know how much it's willing to spend a month and accept that the design will be shaped by that number. If nobody knows it, the surprise arrives with the third month's bill.

Access to production and to the logs. I can't improve what I can't observe. I need to see what people actually asked, what was answered and what failed. Without that, the improvement loop breaks and all that's left is working on hunches.

Accepted time to evaluate, not only to build. If the calendar only covers the demo, the evaluation won't happen; and without evaluation there's no way to know whether the system gets worse when the model changes. That time has to be defended the same way testing time is defended in any other serious project.

en.text: Without strategic prioritisation, companies chase many low-impact applications and end up falsely concluding that AI delivers no return.
en.source: Chip Huyen · Common pitfalls when building generative AI applications, January 2025

en.text: And one idea that really tidies up my conversations with clients: almost none of those eight things is about artificial intelligence. They're written rules, accessible data, a person who decides, and a known budget. In other words, it's the same maturity any software project needs in order to go well. AI doesn't create that problem; what it does is expose it much earlier.
What I'd do if I were starting tomorrow

I'll finish with what I'd tell someone coming from software development who's looking at these ads, which is roughly where I was. You don't start from zero: what you already know is half the road, and it's precisely the half you can't improvise.

items: Don't throw away your trade. Clear contracts, boundaries between layers, tests, observability, cost. All of it is worth more here than before, precisely because the piece in the middle is unreliable.
Learn to evaluate first, not to prompt. Take a small problem of your own, write thirty cases with their expected answers, and measure. That exercise teaches more than twenty tutorials.
Build one whole thing, however tiny. From idea to production, with its measurement and its bill. One finished thing teaches more than five half-done ones, and you can show it in an interview.
Document why you decided each thing. In a field that changes every six months, judgement is the only thing that doesn't expire.
And don't believe any figure without going to the source. Mine included: every number in this note has a link below so you can check it.

en.text: In conclusion

If I had to reduce all of this to a single idea, it would be this: companies aren't looking for someone who knows about artificial intelligence. They're looking for three old things — speed, something that works, and code you can keep extending a year from now without throwing it away — and today AI is the fastest route they've found to get them. AI is in the job title; what's in the hiring manager's head is those three.

And something has changed that's worth saying plainly: hardly anyone reads the code any more. Not the client, not the business side, and often not the colleague who inherits it. What gets looked at is the product: does it answer, is it fast, does it stay up. That can sound like the code no longer matters, and it's exactly the opposite. When nobody looks inside, the inside has to hold on its own, because there's no longer anyone to warn you in time.

items: The clear idea. Before writing a line, knowing what it has to do and how you'll know it does. It's step 2 and step 3 of this note, and it's the only thing separating a product from a demo.
Speed. Shipping early and in pieces you can watch working. Not because there's a rush, but because until something is in front of someone, you don't know whether it's any use.
Standards. Clear contracts, boundaries between layers, tests, logs and a watched budget. It's what stops the speed from the previous point being paid back next month with interest.

en.text: And all three or none, because each pair without the third fails in its own way. Clear idea and standards but no speed: you arrive late. Speed and a clear idea but no standards: you ship something nobody dares touch three months later. Speed and standards but no clear idea: you build, impeccably, something nobody needed. An AI engineer worth hiring is someone who holds all three at once with an unreliable piece at the centre of the system. That's the job. Everything else is vocabulary.

If you're deciding whether this role is for you, my honest summary is this: it isn't a research job in disguise, it's software engineering with an unreliable piece at the centre and an obligation to prove the result is correct anyway. If that sounds like a good problem, you'll enjoy it. You can see what I'm working on in [my projects](/projects) and how I work with companies in [for companies](/for-companies).
Further reading

items: [The Rise of the AI Engineer](https://www.latent.space/p/ai-engineer) — Shawn "swyx" Wang, 2023: the essay that named the role. Worth reading if only to understand where the title in those job ads came from.

[AI Engineering: Building Applications with Foundation Models](https://www.oreilly.com/library/view/ai-engineering/9781098166298/) — Chip Huyen, O'Reilly, 2025: the reference book on building on top of existing models, with the best explanation of evaluation I've read.

[Common pitfalls when building generative AI applications](https://huyenchip.com/2025/01/16/ai-engineering-pitfalls.html) — Chip Huyen, 2025: six expensive mistakes explained in one afternoon. If you read only one thing on this list, make it this one.

[Building effective agents](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/building-effective-agents) — Anthropic, 2024: the guide that recommends starting with the simplest thing and adding complexity only when needed.

[Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.11401) — Lewis, Perez, Piktus and others, NeurIPS 2020: the paper that invented RAG. Read it if anyone ever tells you RAG trains the model.

[Seven Failure Points When Engineering a Retrieval Augmented Generation System](https://arxiv.org/abs/2401.05856) — Barnett and others, 2024: seven documented ways this breaks in production, drawn from real running systems.

[RAG vs. fine-tuning](https://www.redhat.com/en/topics/ai/rag-vs-fine-tuning) — Red Hat: the shortest, clearest explanation of the difference, to forward to whoever asks you for "a RAG to train the model".

[AI engineer · National Careers Service](https://nationalcareers.service.gov.uk/job-profiles/artificial-intelligence-ai-engineer) — the UK public careers service: the official role profile, with tasks, entry routes and salary ranges.

[The U.S. AI Workforce](https://cset.georgetown.edu/wp-content/uploads/US-AI-Workforce_Brief-2.pdf) — CSET, Georgetown University, 2021: the report explaining why there is no official AI occupation and what that implies when measuring the market.

[LinkedIn Jobs on the Rise 2025](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/linkedin-jobs-rise-2025-25-fastest-growing-us-linkedin-news-gryie) — LinkedIn News, January 2025: the 25 fastest-growing jobs in the U.S., with "AI engineer" at number one and the description they themselves use.
[Future of Jobs Report 2025](https://www.weforum.org/publications/the-future-of-jobs-report-2025/) — World Economic Forum: the fastest-growing profiles through 2030, with the methodology on show.
[Stack Overflow Developer Survey 2025](https://survey.stackoverflow.co/2025/ai) — the sector's largest survey: AI usage up, trust down, and the detail on "almost right, but not quite".
